question:
Problem A-6
Let \( \sigma \) be a bijection of the positive integers, that is, a one-to-one function from ( \( 1,2,3, \ldots \) ) onto itself. Let \( x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}, \ldots \) be a sequence of real numbers with the following three properties:
(i) \( \left|x_{n}\right| \) is a strictly decreasing function of \( n \);
(ii) \( |\sigma(n)-n| \cdot\left|x_{n}\right| \rightarrow 0 \) as \( n \rightarrow \infty \);
(iii) \( \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_{k}=1 \).

Prove or disprove that these conditions imply that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{k=1}^{n} x_{\theta(k)}=1
\]
solution:
A-6.
We disprove the assertion. Let \( y_{n}=1 /(n+1) \ln (n+1) \). Then \( \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{n+1} y_{n} \) converges to some \( g>0 \) since \( y_{n} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( n \rightarrow \infty \) and \( y_{1}>y_{2}>\cdots \). Let \( x_{n}=(-1)^{n+1} y_{n} / g \). Then conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Let \( a_{0}, a_{1}, \ldots \) be positive integers to be made more definite later. Let \( b_{0}=0 \) and \( b_{t+1}=b_{i}+4 a_{i} \) for \( i=0,1, \ldots \). The bijection \( \sigma \) is defined as follows:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\sigma(n)=2 n-1-b_{i} \text { for } b_{i}<n \leqslant b_{i}+2 a_{i} \\
\sigma(n)=2 n-b_{i+1} \text { for } b_{i}+2 a_{i}<n \leqslant b_{i+1}
\end{array}
\]

Then \( 0<\sigma(n)<2 n \) and hence \( |\sigma(n)-n|<n \). Thus
\[
|\sigma(n)-n| \cdot\left|x_{n}\right|<\frac{n}{g(n+1) \ln (n+1)}
\]
which implies condition (ii). Let \( C(n)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i} \) and \( D(n)=\sum_{l=1}^{n} x_{\sigma(i)} \). Then
\[
D\left(b_{1}+2 a_{i}\right)-C\left(b_{1}+2 a_{i}\right)=\frac{1}{g} \sum_{j=1}^{a_{1}} y_{b_{1}+2 j}+\frac{1}{g} \sum_{k=1}^{a_{1}} y_{b_{1}+2 a_{i}+2 k-1}
\]

Since \( y_{2}+y_{4}+y_{6}+\cdots \) diverges to \( +\infty \) by the integral test, the \( a_{1} \) can be chosen large enough so that the first sum in (A) exceeds 1 for each \( i \). Then \( D(n)>1+C(n) \) for an unbounded sequence of \( n \) 's. Hence \( D(n) \) and \( C(n) \) cannot converge to the same limit.

descriptive_long.question:
Problem A-6
Let \( permutfn \) be a bijection of the positive integers, that is, a one-to-one function from ( \( 1,2,3, \ldots \) ) onto itself. Let \( seqxval_{1}, seqxval_{2}, seqxval_{3}, \ldots \) be a sequence of real numbers with the following three properties:
(i) \( \left|seqxval_{indexn}\right| \) is a strictly decreasing function of \( indexn \);
(ii) \( |permutfn(indexn)-indexn| \cdot\left|seqxval_{indexn}\right| \rightarrow 0 \) as \( indexn \rightarrow \infty \);
(iii) \( \lim _{indexn \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{indexk=1}^{indexn} seqxval_{indexk}=1 \).

Prove or disprove that these conditions imply that
\[
\lim _{indexn \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{indexk=1}^{indexn} seqxval_{thetafn(indexk)}=1
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
A-6.
We disprove the assertion. Let \( seqyval_{indexn}=1 /\bigl((indexn+1) \ln (indexn+1)\bigr) \). Then \( \sum_{indexn=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{indexn+1} seqyval_{indexn} \) converges to some \( constgval>0 \) since \( seqyval_{indexn} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( indexn \rightarrow \infty \) and \( seqyval_{1}>seqyval_{2}>\cdots \). Let \( seqxval_{indexn}=(-1)^{indexn+1} seqyval_{indexn} / constgval \). Then conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Let \( paramazero, paramaone, \ldots \) be positive integers to be made more definite later. Let \( parambzero=0 \) and \( parambtplusone=parambi+4\,paramai \) for \( indexi=0,1, \ldots \). The bijection \( permutfn \) is defined as follows:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
permutfn(indexn)=2\,indexn-1-parambi \text { for } parambi<indexn \leqslant parambi+2\,paramai \\
permutfn(indexn)=2\,indexn-b_{indexi+1} \text { for } parambi+2\,paramai<indexn \leqslant b_{indexi+1}
\end{array}
\]

Then \( 0<permutfn(indexn)<2\,indexn \) and hence \( |permutfn(indexn)-indexn|<indexn \). Thus
\[
|permutfn(indexn)-indexn| \cdot \left|seqxval_{indexn}\right|<\frac{indexn}{constgval\,(indexn+1) \ln (indexn+1)}
\]
which implies condition (ii). Let \( partialc(indexn)=\sum_{indexi=1}^{indexn} seqxval_{indexi} \) and \( partiald(indexn)=\sum_{indexl=1}^{indexn} seqxval_{permutfn(indexi)} \). Then
\[
partiald\left(b_{1}+2\,paramai\right)-partialc\left(b_{1}+2\,paramai\right)=\frac{1}{constgval} \sum_{j=1}^{paramaone} seqyval_{b_{1}+2 j}+\frac{1}{constgval} \sum_{indexk=1}^{paramaone} seqyval_{b_{1}+2\,paramai+2\,indexk-1}
\]

Since \( seqyval_{2}+seqyval_{4}+seqyval_{6}+\cdots \) diverges to \( +\infty \) by the integral test, the \( paramaone \) can be chosen large enough so that the first sum in (A) exceeds 1 for each \( indexi \). Then \( partiald(indexn)>1+partialc(indexn) \) for an unbounded sequence of \( indexn \)'s. Hence \( partiald(indexn) \) and \( partialc(indexn) \) cannot converge to the same limit.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem A-6
Let \( driftwood \) be a bijection of the positive integers, that is, a one-to-one function from ( \( 1,2,3, \ldots \) ) onto itself. Let \( orchardia_{1}, orchardia_{2}, orchardia_{3}, \ldots \) be a sequence of real numbers with the following three properties:
(i) \( \left|orchardia_{pebblenum}\right| \) is a strictly decreasing function of \( pebblenum \);
(ii) \( |driftwood(pebblenum)-pebblenum| \cdot\left|orchardia_{pebblenum}\right| \rightarrow 0 \) as \( pebblenum \rightarrow \infty \);
(iii) \( \lim _{pebblenum \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{quartzkey=1}^{pebblenum} orchardia_{quartzkey}=1 \).

Prove or disprove that these conditions imply that
\[
\lim _{pebblenum \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{quartzkey=1}^{pebblenum} orchardia_{hemlocker(quartzkey)}=1
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-6.
We disprove the assertion. Let \( lanternfy_{pebblenum}=1 /(pebblenum+1) \ln (pebblenum+1) \). Then \( \sum_{pebblenum=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{pebblenum+1} lanternfy_{pebblenum} \) converges to some \( glacierio>0 \) since \( lanternfy_{pebblenum} \rightarrow 0 \) as \( pebblenum \rightarrow \infty \) and \( lanternfy_{1}>lanternfy_{2}>\cdots \). Let \( orchardia_{pebblenum}=(-1)^{pebblenum+1} lanternfy_{pebblenum} / glacierio \). Then conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Let \( marblezero, marblesolo, \ldots \) be positive integers to be made more definite later. Let \( cobblezero=0 \) and \( cobbleplus=cobbleember+4\, marbleember \) for \( emberite=0,1, \ldots \). The bijection \( driftwood \) is defined as follows:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
driftwood(pebblenum)=2\,pebblenum-1-cobbleember \text { for } cobbleember<pebblenum \leqslant cobbleember+2\, marbleember \\
driftwood(pebblenum)=2\,pebblenum-b_{emberite+1} \text { for } cobbleember+2\, marbleember<pebblenum \leqslant b_{emberite+1}
\end{array}
\]

Then \( 0<driftwood(pebblenum)<2\,pebblenum \) and hence \( |driftwood(pebblenum)-pebblenum|<pebblenum \). Thus
\[
|driftwood(pebblenum)-pebblenum| \cdot\left|orchardia_{pebblenum}\right|<\frac{pebblenum}{glacierio(pebblenum+1) \ln (pebblenum+1)}
\]
which implies condition (ii). Let \( midnight(pebblenum)=\sum_{emberite=1}^{pebblenum} orchardia_{emberite} \) and \( sunshadow(pebblenum)=\sum_{fireneedle=1}^{pebblenum} orchardia_{driftwood(emberite)} \). Then
\[
sunshadow\left(b_{1}+2\, marbleember\right)-midnight\left(b_{1}+2\, marbleember\right)=\frac{1}{glacierio} \sum_{j=1}^{marblesolo} lanternfy_{b_{1}+2 j}+\frac{1}{glacierio} \sum_{quartzkey=1}^{marblesolo} lanternfy_{b_{1}+2\, marbleember+2\, quartzkey-1}
\]

Since \( lanternfy_{2}+lanternfy_{4}+lanternfy_{6}+\cdots \) diverges to \( +\infty \) by the integral test, the \( marblesolo \) can be chosen large enough so that the first sum in (A) exceeds 1 for each \( emberite \). Then \( sunshadow(pebblenum)>1+midnight(pebblenum) \) for an unbounded sequence of \( pebblenum \)'s. Hence \( sunshadow(pebblenum) \) and \( midnight(pebblenum) \) cannot converge to the same limit.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem A-6
Let \( constancy \) be a bijection of the positive integers, that is, a one-to-one function from ( \( 1,2,3, \ldots \) ) onto itself. Let \( x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}, \ldots \) be a sequence of real numbers with the following three properties:
(i) \( \left|dullconstant\right| \) is a strictly decreasing function of \( continuum \);
(ii) \( |constancy(continuum)-continuum| \cdot\left|dullconstant\right| \rightarrow 0 \) as \( continuum \rightarrow \infty \);
(iii) \( \lim _{continuum \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{wholepart=1}^{continuum} x_{wholepart}=1 \).

Prove or disprove that these conditions imply that
\[
\lim _{continuum \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{wholepart=1}^{continuum} x_{vagueness(wholepart)}=1
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-6.
We disprove the assertion. Let \( fixedelement=1 /(continuum+1) \ln (continuum+1) \). Then \( \sum_{continuum=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{continuum+1} fixedelement \) converges to some \( emptiness>0 \) since \( fixedelement \rightarrow 0 \) as \( continuum \rightarrow \infty \) and \( y_{1}>y_{2}>\cdots \). Let \( dullconstant=(-1)^{continuum+1} fixedelement / emptiness \). Then conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Let \( emptystart, vacantone, \ldots \) be positive integers to be made more definite later. Let \( fullzero=0 \) and \( fullnext=fullelem+4 vacantelem \) for \( aggregate=0,1, \ldots \). The bijection \( constancy \) is defined as follows:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
constancy(continuum)=2 continuum-1-fullelem \text { for } fullelem<continuum \leqslant fullelem+2 vacantelem \\
constancy(continuum)=2 continuum-b_{aggregate+1} \text { for } fullelem+2 vacantelem<continuum \leqslant b_{aggregate+1}
\end{array}
\]

Then \( 0<constancy(continuum)<2 continuum \) and hence \( |constancy(continuum)-continuum|<continuum \). Thus
\[
|constancy(continuum)-continuum| \cdot\left|dullconstant\right|<\frac{continuum}{emptiness(continuum+1) \ln (continuum+1)}
\]
which implies condition (ii). Let \( differing(continuum)=\sum_{aggregate=1}^{continuum} x_{aggregate} \) and \( uniformity(continuum)=\sum_{altitude=1}^{continuum} x_{constancy(aggregate)} \). Then
\[
uniformity\left(b_{1}+2 vacantelem\right)-differing\left(b_{1}+2 vacantelem\right)=\frac{1}{emptiness} \sum_{j=1}^{vacantone} y_{b_{1}+2 j}+\frac{1}{emptiness} \sum_{wholepart=1}^{vacantone} y_{b_{1}+2 vacantelem+2 wholepart-1}
\]

Since \( y_{2}+y_{4}+y_{6}+\cdots \) diverges to \( +\infty \) by the integral test, the \( vacantone \) can be chosen large enough so that the first sum in (A) exceeds 1 for each \( aggregate \). Then \( uniformity(continuum)>1+differing(continuum) \) for an unbounded sequence of \( continuum \)'s. Hence \( uniformity(continuum) \) and \( differing(continuum) \) cannot converge to the same limit.

garbled_string.question:
Problem A-6
Let \( oupkyqrs \) be a bijection of the positive integers, that is, a one-to-one function from ( \( 1,2,3, \ldots \) ) onto itself. Let \( x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}, \ldots \) be a sequence of real numbers with the following three properties:
(i) \( \left|qzxwvtnp\right| \) is a strictly decreasing function of \( pfldaqiw \);
(ii) \( |oupkyqrs(pfldaqiw)-pfldaqiw| \cdot\left|qzxwvtnp\right| \rightarrow 0 \) as \( pfldaqiw \rightarrow \infty \);
(iii) \( \lim _{pfldaqiw \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{trbvesmu=1}^{pfldaqiw} x_{trbvesmu}=1 \).

Prove or disprove that these conditions imply that
\[
\lim _{pfldaqiw \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{trbvesmu=1}^{pfldaqiw} x_{bafrmgch(trbvesmu)}=1
\]
garbled_string.solution:
A-6.
We disprove the assertion. Let \( hjgrksla=1 /(pfldaqiw+1) \ln (pfldaqiw+1) \). Then \( \sum_{pfldaqiw=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{pfldaqiw+1} hjgrksla \) converges to some \( esmcxkri>0 \) since \( hjgrksla \rightarrow 0 \) as \( pfldaqiw \rightarrow \infty \) and \( y_{1}>y_{2}>\cdots \). Let \( qzxwvtnp=(-1)^{pfldaqiw+1} hjgrksla / esmcxkri \). Then conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied. Let \( jkduhser, gtypznql, \ldots \) be positive integers to be made more definite later. Let \( yapfukdm=0 \) and \( tqgmsovh=rlvcwjqo+4 navorxke \) for \( wjlxhzgn=0,1, \ldots \). The bijection \( oupkyqrs \) is defined as follows:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
oupkyqrs(pfldaqiw)=2 pfldaqiw-1-rlvcwjqo \text { for } rlvcwjqo<pfldaqiw \leqslant rlvcwjqo+2 navorxke \\
oupkyqrs(pfldaqiw)=2 pfldaqiw-b_{wjlxhzgn+1} \text { for } rlvcwjqo+2 navorxke<pfldaqiw \leqslant b_{wjlxhzgn+1}
\end{array}
\]

Then \( 0<oupkyqrs(pfldaqiw)<2 pfldaqiw \) and hence \( |oupkyqrs(pfldaqiw)-pfldaqiw|<pfldaqiw \). Thus
\[
|oupkyqrs(pfldaqiw)-pfldaqiw| \cdot\left|qzxwvtnp\right|<\frac{pfldaqiw}{esmcxkri(pfldaqiw+1) \ln (pfldaqiw+1)}
\]
which implies condition (ii). Let \( mzoejykl(pfldaqiw)=\sum_{wjlxhzgn=1}^{pfldaqiw} x_{wjlxhzgn} \) and \( shfcgrpo(pfldaqiw)=\sum_{xbvyfnda=1}^{pfldaqiw} x_{oupkyqrs(wjlxhzgn)} \). Then
\[
shfcgrpo\left(b_{1}+2 navorxke\right)-mzoejykl\left(b_{1}+2 navorxke\right)=\frac{1}{esmcxkri} \sum_{j=1}^{gtypznql} y_{b_{1}+2 j}+\frac{1}{esmcxkri} \sum_{trbvesmu=1}^{gtypznql} y_{b_{1}+2 navorxke+2 trbvesmu-1}
\]

Since \( y_{2}+y_{4}+y_{6}+\cdots \) diverges to \( +\infty \) by the integral test, the \( gtypznql \) can be chosen large enough so that the first sum in (A) exceeds 1 for each \( wjlxhzgn \). Then \( shfcgrpo(pfldaqiw)>1+mzoejykl(pfldaqiw) \) for an unbounded sequence of \( pfldaqiw \)'s. Hence \( shfcgrpo(pfldaqiw) \) and \( mzoejykl(pfldaqiw) \) cannot converge to the same limit.

kernel_variant.question:
Let \(\sigma\colon\Bbb N\to\Bbb N\) be a bijection.  Suppose that a real sequence \((x_n)_{n\ge 1}\) satisfies
(i) \( |x_{n+1}|<|x_n| \) for every \(n\),
(ii) \( \bigl(|\sigma(n)-n|\bigr)^{1/2}\,|x_n|\longrightarrow 0 \) as \( n\to\infty\),
(iii) \(\displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{k=1}^{n}x_k=1.\)
Does it necessarily follow that
\[\lim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{k=1}^{n}x_{\sigma(k)}=1\ ?\]
Prove your answer.
kernel_variant.solution:
We shall exhibit a bijection \sigma  and a real sequence x_n satisfying (i),(ii),(iii) but for which \sum _{k=1}^n x_{\sigma (k)} does not tend to 1.  The construction follows the official A-6 counterexample.

1.  Define
   y_n = 1\bigl/\bigl((n+1)\sqrt{\ln(n+1)}\bigr),  n\ge1.
Since y_n>0, y_n\to 0, and y_1>y_2>\cdots , the alternating series \sum _{n=1}^\infty  (-1)^{n+1}y_n converges by the Leibniz test.  On the other hand, by the integral test
   \sum _{j=1}^\infty  y_{2j} = \sum _{j=1}^\infty  \frac1{(2j+1)\sqrt{\ln(2j+1)}} = \infty .
Call the limit of the alternating series g>0, and set
   x_n = \frac{(-1)^{n+1}y_n}{g}.
Then |x_n|=y_n/g strictly decreases to 0, and
   \sum _{k=1}^n x_k \to  1
as n\to \infty .  Thus (i) and (iii) hold.

2.  Choose any strictly increasing integers a_0<a_1<a_2<\cdots , and put b_0=0,  b_{t+1}=b_t+4a_t.  We define \sigma  on the t-th block {b_t+1,\ldots ,b_t+4a_t} by
  *  for b_t<n\leq b_t+2a_t,   \sigma (n)=2n-1-b_t,
  *  for b_t+2a_t<n\leq b_t+4a_t,   \sigma (n)=2n-(b_t+4a_t).
One easily checks that this gives a permutation of \mathbb{N}, and that the image of the t-th block is again {b_t+1,\ldots ,b_t+4a_t}.

3.  Verification of (ii):  Since 0<\sigma (n)<2n one has |\sigma (n)-n|<n, so
   (|\sigma (n)-n|)^{1/2}|x_n| \leq  \sqrt{n}\cdot y_n/g = \frac{\sqrt{n}}{g\,(n+1)\sqrt{\ln(n+1)}}
which tends to 0.  Hence (ii) holds.

4.  Finally set
   C(n)=\sum _{k=1}^n x_k,   D(n)=\sum _{k=1}^n x_{\sigma (k)}.
Fix t and let N=b_t+2a_t (the midpoint of the t-th block).  One checks by listing which x_k get moved forward or backward that

  D(N)-C(N)
  = \frac1g \Bigl( \sum _{j=1}^{a_t}y_{b_t+2j}  
                  +\sum _{j=1}^{a_t}y_{b_t+2a_t+2j-1}\Bigr).

Because \sum _{j=1}^\infty  y_{b_t+2j}=\infty , we may choose a_t so large that
   \sum _{j=1}^{a_t}y_{b_t+2j} > 2.
Then D(N)-C(N)>2/g, so for those N we have D(N)>C(N)+2/g.  But C(N)\to 1, so the values D(N) cannot approach 1.  Hence \sum _{k=1}^n x_{\sigma (k)} does not converge to 1.

This completes the counterexample, showing that (i)-(iii) need not force \sum _{k=1}^n x_{\sigma (k)}\to 1.

core_steps: Pick a conditionally convergent alternating series whose positive (or negative) subseries diverges
Scale the series so its standard partial sums approach 1
Design a block-wise permutation that drags many same-sign terms forward while keeping |σ(n)−n|·|x_n|→0
Verify displacement condition (ii) via a crude upper bound on |σ(n)−n| and the slow decay of |x_n|
Use the divergence of the grouped subseries to force the permuted partial sums away from 1 infinitely often

slot1.description: Concrete choice of the slowly varying positive terms that make the alternating series conditionally convergent but whose even-indexed sum diverges

slot2.description: Numerical constant controlling block length in the definition of b_{t+1} = b_t + (constant)·a_t
